name: InfiniBand Architecture
description: Core InfiniBand architecture including protocol layers, addressing, and fabric management
key_facts: InfiniBand uses a layered architecture with Physical, Link, Network, and Transport layers
Each device on the InfiniBand fabric has a unique GUID (Globally Unique Identifier) and is assigned a LID (Local ID) by the Subnet Manager
InfiniBand subnets are managed by a Subnet Manager (SM) that configures routing and assigns addresses
Queue Pairs (QPs) are the fundamental communication abstraction in InfiniBand, providing reliable or unreliable connections
NVIDIA Quantum-2 InfiniBand switches support NDR (400 Gb/s) speeds with adaptive routing
common_misconceptions: InfiniBand addressing works like IP networking (IB uses LIDs for intra-subnet and GIDs for inter-subnet routing)
InfiniBand requires manual routing configuration (the Subnet Manager automatically computes and distributes routing tables)

name: InfiniBand Subnet Management
description: Subnet Manager configuration, high availability, and fabric discovery
key_facts: OpenSM is the open-source Subnet Manager implementation commonly used with NVIDIA InfiniBand
The SM performs fabric discovery, LID assignment, routing table computation, and distribution
Standby SMs provide high availability; they take over if the active SM fails
SM priority determines which SM becomes active when multiple SMs are present on the fabric
Periodic light sweeps detect topology changes without performing full fabric rediscovery
common_misconceptions: Each switch needs its own Subnet Manager (one SM manages the entire subnet; additional SMs serve as standbys)
SM failover is instantaneous (there is a brief disruption during handover as the new SM performs a sweep)

name: InfiniBand Speed Grades and Encoding
description: InfiniBand speed tiers, lane configurations, and data encoding schemes
key_facts: NDR (Next Data Rate) provides 100 Gb/s per lane with 4x lanes for 400 Gb/s aggregate per port
HDR (High Data Rate) provides 50 Gb/s per lane for 200 Gb/s per port
InfiniBand uses 1x, 4x, and 12x lane widths, with 4x being the most common for AI clusters
64b/66b encoding in NDR provides 97% encoding efficiency, a significant improvement over HDR's PAM4
Effective data throughput is less than signaling rate due to protocol overhead and encoding
common_misconceptions: InfiniBand speed grades are backward compatible without bandwidth impact (mixing speeds requires speed negotiation, reducing to the slower rate)
Doubling the port speed doubles real application throughput (protocol overhead and software bottlenecks limit actual gains)

name: Ethernet Networking for AI
description: Ethernet-based networking solutions for AI infrastructure including high-speed switches and NICs
key_facts: NVIDIA Spectrum-4 Ethernet switches provide 400 GbE and 800 GbE port speeds for AI clusters
NVIDIA ConnectX-7 SmartNICs support 400 GbE with hardware-accelerated RoCE for GPU networking
Priority Flow Control (PFC) enables lossless Ethernet required for RDMA over Converged Ethernet
ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification) helps manage congestion in lossless Ethernet fabrics
Ethernet provides a lower-cost alternative to InfiniBand for inference and smaller training workloads
common_misconceptions: Standard Ethernet switches work for AI training (AI training requires lossless Ethernet with PFC and ECN configuration)
Ethernet can never match InfiniBand performance for AI (with RoCE and proper tuning, Ethernet approaches InfiniBand for many workloads)

name: NVIDIA BlueField DPU
description: NVIDIA BlueField Data Processing Unit for network acceleration and infrastructure offload
key_facts: BlueField DPUs combine a SmartNIC with Arm CPU cores for running infrastructure services independently from the host
DPUs offload networking, storage, and security functions from host CPUs, freeing them for AI compute
BlueField supports hardware-accelerated OVS (Open Virtual Switch) for high-performance virtual networking
DOCA (Data Center Infrastructure on a Chip Architecture) is the SDK for programming BlueField DPUs
BlueField provides hardware root of trust and secure boot for data center security
common_misconceptions: DPUs are just fancy NICs (DPUs have their own OS and compute capabilities independent of the host)
DPUs are only useful for networking (they also accelerate storage, security, and telemetry functions)

name: RDMA Fundamentals
description: Remote Direct Memory Access technology for low-latency, high-throughput data transfer
key_facts: RDMA allows direct memory access between machines without involving the CPU in the data path
RDMA operations include Send/Receive, Read, Write, and Atomic operations
RDMA verbs API (libibverbs) provides the programming interface for RDMA communication
RDMA achieves microsecond-level latency compared to millisecond latency in traditional TCP/IP networking
Memory regions must be registered with the RDMA adapter before they can be used for RDMA operations
common_misconceptions: RDMA eliminates all network overhead (connection setup, memory registration, and completion handling still have overhead)
RDMA is only available over InfiniBand (RoCE enables RDMA over standard Ethernet infrastructure)

name: RoCE Configuration and Deployment
description: Configuring and deploying RDMA over Converged Ethernet for AI workloads
key_facts: RoCEv2 encapsulates RDMA over UDP/IP, enabling routing across L3 networks
PFC (Priority Flow Control) must be configured on both NICs and switches for lossless RoCE operation
DSCP-based QoS maps RDMA traffic to the correct priority queue for proper PFC behavior
ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification) and DCQCN congestion control prevent PFC storms
GPUDirect RDMA with RoCE enables direct NIC-to-GPU memory transfers over Ethernet
common_misconceptions: RoCE works without any special switch configuration (PFC, ECN, and QoS must be configured on all switches in the path)
RoCEv1 and RoCEv2 are interchangeable (RoCEv1 is L2-only while RoCEv2 supports L3 routing)

name: Network Fabric Management
description: Tools and practices for managing AI network infrastructure including NVIDIA UFM
key_facts: NVIDIA UFM (Unified Fabric Manager) provides centralized InfiniBand fabric management and monitoring
UFM performs fabric discovery, health monitoring, event logging, and topology visualization
NVIDIA NetQ provides network monitoring and troubleshooting for Ethernet-based AI networks
Fabric health checks validate link integrity, port errors, and congestion across the network
Firmware management across switches and NICs ensures consistent behavior and security patching
common_misconceptions: Standard network monitoring tools work for InfiniBand (InfiniBand requires specialized tools like UFM that understand IB-specific protocols)
Network management is only needed during initial setup (ongoing monitoring is critical for detecting degradation and failures)

name: Network Telemetry and Analytics
description: Collecting and analyzing network performance data for AI infrastructure
key_facts: In-band telemetry embeds performance data directly into network packets for real-time monitoring
Port counters track bytes, packets, errors, and discards on each switch port
Congestion detection uses ECN marking rates and PFC pause frame counts to identify bottlenecks
Histogram-based latency monitoring provides distribution analysis of network delay
Integration with Prometheus and Grafana enables centralized visualization of network metrics
common_misconceptions: Network monitoring adds significant overhead to AI training (modern telemetry collection has negligible impact on data plane performance)
High bandwidth utilization means the network is healthy (high error rates or latency spikes indicate problems despite high throughput)

name: NCCL and Collective Communication Tuning
description: Tuning NVIDIA Collective Communications Library for optimal distributed training performance
key_facts: NCCL implements optimized collective operations (AllReduce, AllGather, ReduceScatter) for multi-GPU training
NCCL_ALGO selects the communication algorithm (ring, tree, or collnet) based on message size and topology
NCCL_IB_HCA specifies which InfiniBand HCA ports to use for inter-node communication
NCCL topology detection automatically selects optimal communication paths based on GPU and network topology
SHARP offloads collective operations to InfiniBand switches, reducing network traffic and host CPU usage
common_misconceptions: Default NCCL settings are optimal for all configurations (tuning NCCL variables can improve distributed training performance by 10-30%)
NCCL only works with InfiniBand (NCCL supports both InfiniBand and Ethernet with appropriate transports)
